items.q: Is profit the only objective businesses pursue?
items.correct: No — businesses also pursue survival (especially when new), growth, market share, customer satisfaction and social or ethical aims; objectives change over time.
items.misconception: Every business decision is made purely to maximise profit.
items.random_wrong: Businesses are legally required to donate all profit to charity.
items.why: Students default to profit maximisation; recognising survival and social objectives is essential for this unit.

items.q: Are shareholders and stakeholders the same?
items.correct: No — shareholders own part of a company, while stakeholders are anyone affected by it: employees, customers, suppliers, the local community and shareholders too.
items.misconception: Stakeholder is just another word for shareholder.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholders are people who sell wooden stakes for fencing.
items.why: The similar words cause persistent confusion; stakeholders is the much broader group.

items.q: Does unlimited liability mean a sole trader can lose only what they invested?
items.correct: No — unlimited liability means personal assets such as a house or car can be taken to pay business debts; that is the key risk of operating as a sole trader.
items.misconception: A business owner can never lose more than the money they put in.
items.random_wrong: Unlimited liability means a business can borrow unlimited money.
items.why: Students apply limited liability logic to sole traders, missing why incorporation matters.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SMART objectives stand for?
items.why: It's Specific. “Strategic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which 1970 essay argued the 'social responsibility of business is to increase profits'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Friedman. “Drucker” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many elements are in the SMART acronym?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 elements. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
